subtopic: 1.1.1 Structure and Function of the Processor
objectives: Describe the function of the ALU, Control Unit, registers (PC, MAR, MDR, CIR, ACC) and the three system buses
Explain the stages of the fetch-decode-execute cycle and how registers and buses interact during it
Apply the factors affecting CPU performance (clock speed, cores, cache, pipelining) to compare processors for a given scenario

retrieval.q: Which register always holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched?
retrieval.a: The Program Counter (PC), which is incremented each cycle.

retrieval.q: State the difference between the MAR and the MDR.
retrieval.a: The MAR holds the address of a memory location to read/write; the MDR holds the data or instruction itself.

retrieval.q: Is the address bus unidirectional or bidirectional, and in which direction does it carry signals?
retrieval.a: Unidirectional, carrying memory addresses from the CPU to memory only.

retrieval.q: Convert the denary number 25 to 8-bit binary (interleaves 1.2/1.4 number work).
Name one example each of an input and an output device (interleaves 1.1.3).

keywords.def: Arithmetic Logic Unit; performs arithmetic, logic and shift operations, placing the result in the accumulator.
Decodes instructions, generates control signals and synchronises the CPU with the clock.
Program Counter; holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched, incremented each cycle.
Memory Address Register; holds the address of the location to be read from or written to.
Memory Data Register; holds the data or instruction just fetched or about to be written.
Current Instruction Register; holds the current instruction while it is decoded and executed.
Small, very fast memory on or near the CPU holding frequently or recently used data and instructions.
Overlapping the fetch, decode and execute stages of consecutive instructions to increase throughput.

content.heading: Registers and the ALU/CU
points: The ALU carries out arithmetic (+ - x /), logic (AND, OR, NOT, compare) and shift operations, with results sent to the ACC
The Control Unit decodes instructions, generates control signals and synchronises data flow with the clock
Key registers: PC (next address), MAR (address), MDR (data), CIR (current instruction), ACC (immediate ALU result)

content.heading: The three system buses
points: Address bus is unidirectional (CPU to memory); its width sets the maximum addressable locations as 2^n
Data bus is bidirectional and carries data and instructions; its width affects throughput
Control bus is bidirectional and carries control signals such as read/write, clock and interrupt

content.heading: Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle
points: Fetch: PC copied to MAR, read signal on control bus, instruction returns via data bus to MDR, PC incremented, MDR copied to CIR
Decode: the CU splits the CIR contents into opcode (operation) and operand (data/address)
Execute: the operation is carried out, e.g. an ALU calculation (result to ACC), a load/store, or a jump that writes a new address into the PC

content.heading: Factors affecting performance
points: Clock speed: more pulses per second means more FDE cycles per second, but is limited by heat and power
Cores: independent units each running their own FDE, but only faster if the task parallelises and the software supports it
Cache: L1 (fastest/smallest, on core) then L2 then L3 (largest/slowest, often shared), all checked before slower RAM; pipelining overlaps stages for higher throughput

worked_example.title: Tracing the fetch stage of the FDE cycle
steps: The address in the PC is copied into the MAR
A read signal is placed on the control bus; the instruction is returned along the data bus into the MDR
The PC is incremented (PC := PC + 1) so it points to the next instruction
The contents of the MDR are copied into the CIR ready to be decoded
The CU then splits the CIR into opcode and operand (decode) before the operation is executed

task: Complete a labelled FDE cycle trace worksheet: for a short instruction (e.g. LOAD then ADD), write out the register transfers for fetch, decode and execute in order, naming every register and bus involved.
challenge: A CPU has a 16-bit address bus. Calculate the maximum number of addressable locations (showing 2^n working), then explain how doubling the address bus width and adding L2 cache would each affect performance and cost.

exit_ticket.q: Name the two things in the fetch stage that students most often forget.
exit_ticket.a: Incrementing the PC and copying the MDR into the CIR.

exit_ticket.q: Why does adding more cores not always make a program run faster?
exit_ticket.a: Only if the task can be parallelised and the software is written to use multiple cores; sequential tasks see little benefit.

exit_ticket.q: Why can a jump (branch) instruction cause a problem in a pipelined CPU?
exit_ticket.a: It writes a new address into the PC, so prefetched instructions in the pipeline must be flushed/discarded, wasting cycles.
